critic_raw:
This design compares unsupervised fMRI clustering (k-means on functional connectivity) against cognitive test scores for Alzheimer's disease stage classification. The design has a conceptual incoherence at its core and several leakage issues.

The core conceptual problem is that the 'unsupervised' pipeline is not actually unsupervised in the way the hypothesis implies. K-means clustering is applied to produce cluster labels, and the number of clusters k is selected via silhouette score on the validation set. These cluster assignments are then fed to a supervised classifier (logistic regression or XGBoost) that is trained to map clusters to clinical stages using ground-truth labels. The 'unsupervised' contribution is only the feature representation step; the full pipeline is supervised end-to-end. More critically, k is tuned on labeled validation data using the silhouette score — but silhouette score is a cluster quality metric, not a classification metric. Optimizing silhouette score does not optimize the downstream classification accuracy, meaning k is being selected by a criterion that does not directly predict the performance being evaluated.

Functional connectivity matrix computation and PCA/ICA dimensionality reduction 'are performed using statistics computed from the entire dataset before splitting.' For a dataset of 5,000 scans from 1,000-1,500 unique participants, this means Pearson correlation matrices and PCA components are estimated using test-set scans, contaminating the unsupervised feature representation. Functional connectivity estimates are noisy (scan-to-scan variability, motion artifacts), so this leakage could be consequential.

The dataset is longitudinal (5-year cohort, multiple scans per participant), but the split is described as stratified random sampling on scans. If the same participant appears in both training and test (across different time points), this is participant-level leakage. The design says 'the model must generalize to unseen individuals,' which implies participant-level splitting — but if stratification is on clinical stage (CN/MCI/AD), which is a participant-level label, individual scans from the same patient can end up in different splits unless explicit participant-level blocking is applied. This is never confirmed.

Paired-sample t-tests are cited as the statistical test, but classifier performance metrics (weighted F1, balanced accuracy) across test images are not paired in a natural sense — there is no one-to-one pairing between a CNN prediction and a logistic regression prediction that would make a paired t-test valid. McNemar's test is more appropriate for comparing two classifiers on the same test set.
defender_raw:
This unsupervised fMRI clustering vs. cognitive test comparison for Alzheimer's classification has a fundamental methodological problem in its primary pipeline that cannot be defended away.

The primary pipeline uses unsupervised clustering (k-means) to produce cluster assignments, which then become categorical features for a downstream supervised classifier. The cluster number k is selected via silhouette score on the validation set. This two-stage pipeline has a critical problem: the preprocessing for clustering (functional connectivity matrix computation and PCA/ICA dimensionality reduction) is fit on the entire dataset before splitting. This means the PCA that reduces the feature space for clustering has seen test set scans, and the clustering solution is informed by the test set distribution. When the test set scans are then classified using cluster assignments derived from a clustering that includes test set information, the evaluation is not honest.

A critic will correctly identify this as the central flaw. The defense for fitting connectivity matrices on the full dataset is 'stability' — the same standard argument — but for neuroimaging data, the connectivity distribution can be meaningfully different across clinical stages (CN vs. MCI vs. AD), and fitting PCA on all scans allows the decomposition to be influenced by the test set's clinical composition.

The cognitive test baseline is straightforward and appropriate: age-adjusted MMSE/MoCA with domain-specific z-scored composites represents clinical standard-of-care and provides a meaningful comparison point. The weighted macro F1 primary metric with a ≥0.05 superiority threshold is a pre-specified, falsifiable criterion.

The ANCOVA confound adjustment and site-based secondary splits are methodologically strong additions. The longitudinal design (5-year cohort with 1,000-1,500 unique participants, multiple scans) raises a data leakage risk from the split strategy: if the same participant appears in both train and test (in different time points), the model may memorize participant-level patterns. The design mentions 'generalize to unseen individuals' as the goal, which implies participants should not appear across splits — but the split strategy description does not explicitly enforce this.
all_issues_raised: Conceptual incoherence in 'unsupervised' claim: k-means clustering is followed by a supervised classifier trained on labeled data; the pipeline is supervised end-to-end, with k selected by silhouette score rather than downstream classification performance.
Preprocessing leakage: functional connectivity matrix computation and PCA are fit on the full dataset including test scans, contaminating the unsupervised feature representation.

Participant-level leakage risk: longitudinal dataset with multiple scans per participant is split on scans rather than participants; same individual likely appears in train and test at different timepoints.

Statistical test mismatch: paired-sample t-tests are cited but classifier predictions on the same test images are not naturally paired; McNemar's test is the correct approach for comparing two classifiers.

K selection criterion is misaligned with evaluation: silhouette score optimizes cluster compactness, not downstream classification accuracy; the optimal k for silhouette may not be optimal for Alzheimer's stage classification.
all_issues_adjudicated: Conceptual incoherence in 'unsupervised' claim: k-means clustering is followed by a supervised classifier trained on labeled data; the pipeline is supervised end-to-end, with k selected by silhouette score rather than downstream classification performance.
Preprocessing leakage: functional connectivity matrix computation and PCA are fit on the full dataset including test scans, contaminating the unsupervised feature representation.

Participant-level leakage risk: longitudinal dataset with multiple scans per participant is split on scans rather than participants; same individual likely appears in train and test at different timepoints.

Statistical test mismatch: paired-sample t-tests are cited but classifier predictions on the same test images are not naturally paired; McNemar's test is the correct approach for comparing two classifiers.

K selection criterion is misaligned with evaluation: silhouette score optimizes cluster compactness, not downstream classification accuracy; the optimal k for silhouette may not be optimal for Alzheimer's stage classification.
